meta.title: Euclid's Theorem
meta.subject: Euclid's theorem — why no finite list can exhaust the primes

scenes.title: Euclid's Theorem
scenes.tagline: Primes do not run out because every finite list can be escaped
scenes.footnote: Wikipedia source page   ·   reviewed as an idea, not a proof catalog   ·   surveyed past the common overclaim

beats.narration: The page says there are at least 200 proofs that primes never end. This film is about the one move that still carries the load: Euclid's original escape from any finite list.
The misconception to kill is double. Euclid did not start by assuming he already had all primes, and he did not need product plus 1 to be the next prime.
So the job is not to tour a museum of proofs. It is to test what this theorem actually buys, where its elegance comes from, and where that elegance stops.

nodes.label: Prime factor of q
nodes.sub: used only when q is composite

nodes.label: Prime outside the list
nodes.sub: either q itself or a factor of q

beats.narration: Start with any finite list of primes. Euclid folds that whole list into one object, the product P, and then steps one unit away from it to q equals P plus 1.
Now the divisibility trap closes. Any listed prime already divides P. If one also divided q, it would have to divide q minus P, which is 1. No prime can do that.
That is why the theorem survives both branches. If q is prime, q itself is outside the list. If q is composite, any prime factor of q must be outside the list.
And because the starting list was arbitrary, this is not one lucky escape. No finite list can close the primes.

actors.label: Prime factor
actors.sub: used only if q is composite

beats.narration: First compress the proposed list into one product.
Then step away from every divisor in the list by adding 1.
If q is prime, the proof is finished at once: q was not on the list you started with.
If q is composite, the proof does not break. Take a prime factor of q instead.
That factor cannot belong to the original list, because no listed prime can divide both P and P plus 1.
Either branch lands at the same destination: the list was finite, yet not complete.

scenes.heading: What people retell versus what the page says

cells.text: suppose the list already contains all primes
take any finite list of primes
P + 1 itself is the new prime
P + 1 or one of its prime factors is new

beats.narration: The first repair is the starting line. Euclid does not need all primes on the table. Any finite list is enough.
The second repair is the logic. Because q may be prime or composite, the page is right to call this a proof by cases, not contradiction theater for its own sake.
The third repair is the output. The theorem never needs q itself to be prime. A new factor outside the list is enough.

scenes.heading: Euclid proves existence; later theorems add location and density

metrics.label: Guaranteed new primes per finite list

beats.narration: The page starts with scale: at least 200 proofs are known. That tells you the theorem is durable, not that Euclid's own version says everything worth saying.
Euclid spends that abundance on the smallest possible promise. From any finite list, he guarantees 1 more prime outside it.
Bertrand-Chebyshev is already stronger. For every n greater than 1, it places a prime between n and 2n. Now the question is location, not mere existence.
And the prime number theorem goes further still: pi of x grows like x over log x. Euclid proves infinity; later number theory starts measuring how thickly that infinity arrives.

scenes.heading: This proof chooses elegance over census power

nodes.label: Euler and the stronger results
nodes.sub: where primes lie, and how many there are

nodes.label: Risk — overread as a generator
nodes.sub: q may be composite, so the next prime is not handed to you

nodes.label: Why choose it
nodes.sub: the mechanism is visible in one remainder: q minus P equals 1

nodes.label: Risk — no map of the primes
nodes.sub: it cannot say where the next prime lies or how dense the primes are

beats.narration: Here is the trade-off. This account chooses Euclid's elementary escape proof over Euler's richer view because Euclid exposes the mechanism with almost no machinery.
The cost is real. Euclid proves existence, not generation: q may be composite, so the argument can force a new prime factor without handing you the next prime number itself.

And it cannot locate the new prime either. To ask where primes lie, or how many live below x, is to leave Euclid's theorem and enter Bertrand-Chebyshev or the prime number theorem. Elegance here is purchased by narrowness.

scenes.heading: Euclid's theorem — an honest verdict
points: Disposition — decisive as a first proof that primes never run out.
Strongest move — any finite list is escaped because no listed prime divides both P and P + 1.
Common overclaim — the proof is not contradiction theater, and it does not promise P + 1 itself is prime.
Residual limit — it proves existence, not where the next prime lies or how densely primes occur.
Carry this skepticism — ask whether a proof found the new prime itself, or only a factor outside the list.

beats.narration: My ruling is favorable but narrow. As a first explanation of why primes do not run out, Euclid's theorem is decisive.
Its core move is exceptionally clean: any proposed finite list is escaped because a listed prime cannot divide both the product and the product plus 1.
The biggest repair viewers should make to common retellings is twofold. This is not contradiction theater, and it does not say the product plus 1 is always prime.
Its limit is equally clear. The theorem cannot tell you where the next prime lives, and it cannot tell you how densely primes persist.
So the durable skepticism is this: admire Euclid's proof for what it actually buys, and doubt any retelling that lends it a generator or a census it never had.
